1.pericope_label: If the Lord Had Not Been with Us
1.synthesis: Psalm 124 is a thanksgiving psalm built on a sustained hypothetical: what would have happened had YHWH not intervened on Israel’s behalf. The opening summons the community to the shared imaginative exercise: “If it had not been the Lord who was on our side—let Israel now say—if it had not been the Lord who was on our side when people rose up against us, then they would have swallowed us up alive” (Ps 124:1–3). The images of near-disaster accumulate: the flood that would have swept over them, the torrent that would have gone over their soul, the raging waters that would have overwhelmed them (124:4–5). Then the declaration of relief: “Blessed be the Lord, who has not given us as prey to their teeth! We have escaped like a bird from the snare of the fowlers; the snare is broken, and we have escaped!” (124:6–7). The closing grounds the escape in the character of the Helper: “Our help is in the name of the Lord, who made heaven and earth” (124:8).
1.historical_context: Psalm 124 is a Davidic Song of Ascents whose precise historical occasion is unknown, though the imagery of enemies who would have “swallowed us alive” and waters that would have overwhelmed suggests a major military or national crisis narrowly averted. The fourfold use of “if it had not been” / “then” constructs a counterfactual world in which YHWH was absent, then steps back from that world with gratitude. The flood imagery (124:4–5) draws on the ancient Near Eastern association of surging waters with chaos and destruction, echoing the Exodus motif of the sea and YHWH’s mastery over it. The bird-and-snare metaphor (124:7) shifts the imagery from military threat to hunting: Israel is the captured bird whose cage has suddenly broken open. The closing formula—“our help is in the name of the Lord, who made heaven and earth” (124:8)—echoes the confession of Psalm 121 and serves as a kind of liturgical refrain in the Ascent collection.
1.literary_structure: Psalm 124 is organized by its counterfactual structure: three “then” clauses (vv. 3–5) describe what would have happened without YHWH, and the pivot to blessed declaration in verse 6 marks the turn from imagination to reality. The flood imagery of verses 4–5 escalates: first a “flood” that would have swept them away, then a “torrent” going over their soul, then “raging waters” overwhelming. The escalating water language mirrors the imagination of disaster growing before the mind’s eye—each image more overwhelming than the last—until verse 6 breaks it off with “blessed be the Lord.” The bird metaphor (124:7) provides the final image of rescue: not the dramatic parting of waters but the simple breaking of a snare, after which the bird can simply fly. The closing formula anchors the rescue in creation itself: the one who made everything was more than sufficient for this crisis.
1.theological_application: The communal recitation invited by “let Israel now say” (124:1) is a liturgical act of testimony: the congregation is being gathered into a shared acknowledgment that their existence is not self-generated but given. Communities shaped by Psalm 124 practice the discipline of counterfactual gratitude—the deliberate exercise of imagining what would have been without YHWH’s intervention, so that the reality of deliverance is felt rather than taken for granted. The snare that is broken (124:7) is the image of captivity suddenly ended: the community that escaped is the community that must now fly, not sit in the broken cage mourning its former captivity. Deliverance is the beginning of movement, not merely the end of threat.
1.christology: “Our help is in the name of the Lord, who made heaven and earth” (124:8) is the confession of creational sovereignty that Paul grounds in Christ: “for from him and through him and to him are all things” (Rom 11:36). The bird whose snare is broken (124:7) anticipates the liberation Christ announces: “So if the Son sets you free, you will be free indeed” (John 8:36). The flood that would have overwhelmed if YHWH had not been present echoes the disciples’ terror in the storm and Christ’s stilling of it (Mark 4:39)—the one who made heaven and earth and who calms the sea is the same help to whom the psalmist ascribes Israel’s survival. The resurrection is the ultimate expression of YHWH being “on our side”: what would have become of humanity if Christ had not been raised is the counterfactual Paul raises and answers: “if Christ has not been raised, your faith is futile and you are still in your sins” (1 Cor 15:17).
